Clustering

In [24]:
load('.RData')
In [131]:
library(factoextra)
library(tidyverse)
library(NbClust)
library(ggpubr)
library(cluster)
library(dendextend)

In [27]:
# Utilizamos el dataset con los casos con NA values eliminados
df <- na.omit(df_penguins)
df <- as.data.frame(df)
rownames(df) <- paste0(paste0(paste0(rownames(df), substr(df$species,1,1)), substr(df$island,1,1)), substr(df$sex,1,1))
head(df)

1. Clustering distancia

In [28]:
#escalado de los datos
df.sc <- scale(df[, 3:6])
#calculamos distancia euclideana
dist.eucl <- dist(df.sc, method = 'euclidean')
round(as.matrix(dist.eucl)[1:6, 1:6], 2) # Visualizamos solo algunos datos

########### SE SUELE USAR PARA CALCULAR DISTANCIAS IN GENE EXPRESSION

In [29]:
fviz_dist(dist.eucl)
#ROJO alta similitud, AZUL baja similitud

2. K-means

In [30]:
fviz_nbclust(df.sc, kmeans, method = 'wss')
In [31]:
nb <- NbClust(df.sc, distance = 'euclidean', min.nc = 2, max.nc = 12, method = 'kmeans')

# El número de clusters propuestos con mayoria son K = 2, 3, 6

*** : The Hubert index is a graphical method of determining the number of clusters.
                In the plot of Hubert index, we seek a significant knee that corresponds to a 
                significant increase of the value of the measure i.e the significant peak in Hubert
                index second differences plot. 
 

*** : The D index is a graphical method of determining the number of clusters. 
                In the plot of D index, we seek a significant knee (the significant peak in Dindex
                second differences plot) that corresponds to a significant increase of the value of
                the measure. 
 
******************************************************************* 
* Among all indices:                                                
* 9 proposed 2 as the best number of clusters 
* 6 proposed 3 as the best number of clusters 
* 1 proposed 4 as the best number of clusters 
* 1 proposed 5 as the best number of clusters 
* 5 proposed 6 as the best number of clusters 
* 1 proposed 11 as the best number of clusters 
* 1 proposed 12 as the best number of clusters 

                   ***** Conclusion *****                            
 
* According to the majority rule, the best number of clusters is  2 
 
 
*******************************************************************
In [32]:
## k = 2
k2 <- kmeans(df.sc, 2, nstart = 25)
fviz_cluster(k2, data = df.sc,
            repel = TRUE, # Avoid label overplotting (slow)
            ggtheme = theme_minimal())

Warning message:
“ggrepel: 295 unlabeled data points (too many overlaps). Consider increasing max.overlaps”

k3 <- kmeans(df.sc, 3, nstart = 25)
fviz_cluster(k3, data = df.sc,
            repel = TRUE, # Avoid label overplotting (slow)
            ggtheme = theme_minimal())

Warning message:
“ggrepel: 295 unlabeled data points (too many overlaps). Consider increasing max.overlaps”

In [35]:
df_cluster <- cbind(df, data.frame(k2$cluster))
df_cluster <- cbind(df_cluster, data.frame(k3$cluster))
df_cluster <- cbind(df_cluster, data.frame(k6$cluster))
head(df_cluster)
A data.frame: 6 × 11

In [36]:
# 2 Clusters

p2 <- ggplot(df_cluster, aes(k2.cluster, fill = species)) +
        geom_bar(stat = 'count', width=0.7, position=position_dodge()) +
        theme_bw() +
        scale_fill_manual(values = penguins_color) +
        theme(legend.position="top")

p2.sex <- ggplot(df_cluster, aes(k2.cluster, fill = sex)) +
        geom_bar(stat = 'count', width=0.7, position=position_dodge()) +
        theme_bw() +
        scale_fill_manual(values = sex_color) +
        theme(legend.position="top")

ggarrange(p2, p2.sex)
In [88]:
# 3 Clusters
p3 <- ggplot(df_cluster, aes(factor(k3.cluster, levels = c(2, 3, 1)), fill = species)) +
        geom_bar(stat = 'count', width=0.7, position=position_dodge()) +
        labs( x = 'k3.cluster') +
        theme_bw() +
        scale_fill_manual(values = penguins_color) +
        theme(legend.position="top", axis.text.x=element_blank()) +
        coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 150))

p3.sex <- ggplot(df_cluster, aes(factor(k3.cluster, levels = c(2, 3, 1)), fill = sex)) +
        geom_bar(stat = 'count', width=0.7, position=position_dodge()) +
        labs( x = 'k3.cluster') +
        theme_bw() +
        scale_fill_manual(values = sex_color) +
        theme(legend.position="top" , axis.text.x=element_blank())

ggarrange(p3, p3.sex)
In [64]:
# 6 Clusters
p6 <- ggplot(df_cluster, aes(factor(k6.cluster, levels = c(2, 6, 4, 3, 1, 5)), fill = species)) +
        geom_bar(stat = 'count', width=0.7, position=position_dodge()) +
        labs( x = 'k6.cluster') +
        theme_bw() +
        scale_fill_manual(values = penguins_color) +
        theme(legend.position="top", axis.text.x=element_blank()) +
        coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 90))

p6.sex <- ggplot(df_cluster, aes(factor(k6.cluster, levels = c(2, 6, 4, 3, 1, 5)), fill = sex)) +
        geom_bar(stat = 'count', width=0.7, position=position_dodge()) +
        labs( x = 'k6.cluster') +
        theme_bw() +
        scale_fill_manual(values = sex_color) +
        theme(legend.position="top" , axis.text.x=element_blank())

ggarrange(p6, p6.sex)

3. K-Medoids

K-means es muy sessible a outliers, K-medoids es más robusto

In [39]:
fviz_nbclust(df.sc, pam, method = 'silhouette')
In [40]:
pam2 <- pam(df.sc, 2)
fviz_cluster(pam2,
    ellipse.type = "t", 
    repel = TRUE, 
    ggtheme = theme_classic()
)

Warning message:
“ggrepel: 295 unlabeled data points (too many overlaps). Consider increasing max.overlaps”
In [41]:
pam3 <- pam(df.sc, 3)
fviz_cluster(pam3,
    ellipse.type = "t", 
    repel = TRUE, 
    ggtheme = theme_classic()
)

Warning message:
“ggrepel: 296 unlabeled data points (too many overlaps). Consider increasing max.overlaps”
In [42]:
pam6 <- pam(df.sc, 6)
fviz_cluster(pam6,
    ellipse.type = "t", 
    repel = TRUE, 
    ggtheme = theme_classic()
)

Warning message:
“ggrepel: 295 unlabeled data points (too many overlaps). Consider increasing max.overlaps”

4. Comparacion k-means vs k-medoids

In [48]:
# 2 clusters
ggarrange(
    ggarrange(p2, pa2, common.legend = TRUE, legend="top"),
    ggarrange(p2.sex, pa2.sex, common.legend = TRUE, legend="top"),
    nrow = 2, ncol = 1
)
In [89]:
# 3 clusters
ggarrange(
    ggarrange(p3, pa3, common.legend = TRUE, legend="top"),
    ggarrange(p3.sex, pa3.sex, common.legend = TRUE, legend="top"),
    nrow = 2, ncol = 1
)
In [70]:
# 6 clusters
ggarrange(
    ggarrange(p6, pa6, common.legend = TRUE, legend="top"),
    ggarrange(p6.sex, pa6.sex, common.legend = TRUE, legend="top"),
    nrow = 2, ncol = 1
)

Hierarchical Clustering

Clusters Aglomerativos

In [102]:
head(df.sc)
dist.eucl <- dist(df.sc, method = 'euclidean')
res.hc <- hclust(d = dist.eucl, method = 'ward.D2')
res.hc.ave <- hclust(d = dist.eucl, method = 'average')
res.hc.com <- hclust(d = dist.eucl, method = 'complete')
res.hc.sin <- hclust(d = dist.eucl, method = 'single')
A matrix: 6 × 4 of type dbl
bill_length_mmbill_depth_mmflipper_length_mmbody_mass_g
1ATm-0.89469550.7795590-1.4246077-0.5676206
2ATf-0.82155150.1194043-1.0678666-0.5055254
3ATf-0.67526360.4240910-0.4257325-1.1885721
4ATf-1.33355921.0842457-0.5684290-0.9401915
5ATm-0.85812351.7444004-0.7824736-0.6918109
6ATf-0.93126740.3225288-1.4246077-0.7228585
In [103]:
#Verificar el arbol del cluster
res.coph <- cophenetic(res.hc)
cor(dist.eucl, res.coph)

res.coph.ave <- cophenetic(res.hc.ave)
cor(dist.eucl, res.coph.ave)
res.coph.com <- cophenetic(res.hc.com)
cor(dist.eucl, res.coph.com)
res.coph.sin <- cophenetic(res.hc.sin)
cor(dist.eucl, res.coph.sin)

#Se ajustan todos bastantes
0.834462508212719
0.843559266512735
0.826937281889494
0.812449824432909

        cex = 0.5, 
        k_colors = c("#2E9FDF", "#00AFBB", "#E7B800"),
        color_labels_by_k = TRUE, 
        rect = TRUE 
)

Warning message:
“`guides(<scale> = FALSE)` is deprecated. Please use `guides(<scale> = "none")` instead.”
In [119]:
fviz_cluster(list(data = df.sc, cluster = grp),
        ellipse.type = "convex",
        repel = TRUE,
        show.clust.cent = FALSE, ggtheme = theme_minimal())

Warning message:
“ggrepel: 295 unlabeled data points (too many overlaps). Consider increasing max.overlaps”
In [126]:
##Comparar dos dendrogramas
df_sample <- sample_n(df, 30)
df_sample.sc <- scale(df_sample[, 3:6])
res.dist <- dist(df_sample.sc, method = 'euclidean')
fviz_dist(res.dist)
In [138]:
hc1 <- hclust(res.dist, method = 'average')
hc2 <- hclust(res.dist, method = 'ward.D2')
dend1 <- as.dendrogram(hc1)
dend2 <- as.dendrogram(hc2)

dend_list <- dendlist(dend1, dend2)

grp1 <- cutree(hc1, k = 3)
grp2 <- cutree(hc2, k = 3)

fviz_cluster(list(data = df_sample.sc, cluster = grp1),
        ellipse.type = "convex",
        repel = TRUE,
        show.clust.cent = FALSE, ggtheme = theme_minimal())

fviz_cluster(list(data = df_sample.sc, cluster = grp2),
        ellipse.type = "convex",
        repel = TRUE,
        show.clust.cent = FALSE, ggtheme = theme_minimal())
In [134]:
tanglegram(dend1, dend2,
        highlight_distinct_edges = FALSE, # Turn-off dashed lines
        common_subtrees_color_lines = FALSE, # Turn-off line colors
        common_subtrees_color_branches = TRUE, # Color common branches
        main = paste("entanglement =", round(entanglement(dend_list), 2))
)
In [135]:
# Cophenetic correlation matrix
cor.dendlist(dend_list, method = "cophenetic")
A matrix: 2 × 2 of type dbl
1.0000000.958311
0.9583111.000000

save.image()
